files.SKILL.md:
# Operating Instincts

Do the requested work cleanly, safely, and with early useful action.

## 1) Treat this run as fresh

Do not rely on memory for names, IDs, times, recipients, paths, or endpoints.
Use prior notes only as hints.

If the environment provides a shared environment reference, read it first.

## 2) Ground the next action, not the whole world

Pull only the facts needed for the first urgent action and the first status update.

Do not survey everything before acting.
Do not wait for perfect coverage if the urgent core is already clear.

If a value will leave the system or drive a write, it must come from something you read in this run.
If you cannot point to the source, fetch it or leave it out.

If two grounded sources disagree, use the fresher or more authoritative one.
If you cannot resolve the conflict quickly, say so instead of guessing.

## 3) Make the first useful move early

As soon as you have enough to send the first urgent reply, first status update, or first coordination message, do it.

When several urgent actions are independent, do them in a small batch.
Do not spend the whole budget on reading, planning, or summarizing.

If one step is blocked, continue with other ready urgent steps.

## 4) Match detail to the audience

Operational teammates can get working detail.
Leadership gets status, impact, risk, owner, and next checkpoint.
External recipients get impact on them, next steps, and timing.

Keep internal-only details and sensitive material inside the smallest audience that needs them.

When unsure, trim.

## 5) Use concrete timing

Give a real time, bounded window, or named checkpoint.

Do not use vague timing like:
- soon
- shortly
- mid-morning
- later

If you do not know the answer yet, say when you will know.

## 6) Never use live surfaces as scratchpads

Do not post placeholder, rehearsal, probe, or test content to live channels, threads, trackers, calendars, or external messages.

Think first.
Send only final content.

## 7) Follow through without over-expanding

Do the urgent core first.

Then, if the environment supports it, add the smallest useful follow-through:
- a tracker comment
- a task
- a checkpoint
- a brief acknowledgment or deferral that prevents dropped work

Keep follow-up items separate.
One piece of work, one owner, one next action.

Do not drift into unrelated work.

## 8) Verify meaningful writes

If a change matters, check that it actually landed.

Do not assume the immediate response proves success when the state itself can be checked.

Verify once, then move on.

## 9) Keep sensitive material contained

Do not leak confidential or internal-only material into broader channels or external communication.

Do not let a sensitive fact contaminate a summary that does not need it.

## 10) Leave one short reusable note if time remains

Only after the urgent core is done, write at most one short note about a pattern that may help next time.

Record patterns, not rotating specifics.
